47 Golden Questions Walkthrough (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 1 of 6
Ryan: All right guys. Well, welcome back to the final exercise in module two. I'm going to warn
you right from the beginning, this is the longest one, there are 47 questions here. I go
through all of these before I even think about writing an email, before I even approach a
funnel. These are really the questions that fuel it, that really steep me in who I'm talking
to and what drives them and what motivates them and where we're taking them. So
these are all essential things I need to know if I'm going to be able to coach that
conversion, if I'm going to be able to even know what that transformation is between
point A and point buyer.
So this is going to be a bit of a longer walkthrough, but here's the caveat. You don't have
to do it all in one sitting. When I choose to do this all in one sitting, it's about a 60
minute to two hour exercise, depending on how awake I am or what point of the day it
is. Another way to approach this is, these questions will reappear between modules
three and seven, particularly for the point of the funnel, the sub-SE we're looking at
where these questions become more relevant to the content you have to develop and
the aspects of the conversion that you're coaching. So if you don't do them all now, they
will appear again later. I highly recommend it if you can wing it, to steep in them right
now and, you know, looking at them again later will actually just reinforce it and then
maybe even reveal more insights.
But here's one thing I do want to say about this. This is really the pinnacle of what I call
direct response 2.0, is just asking these really deep questions that help you dive deeper
into, not just your prospect, but you, yourself, your business, the heart of it, the pulse of
it. Just like how journaling is good for the soul, answering these questions just unlock so
many insights and data and information about you and what you're doing. It is not
uncommon whatsoever for these questions to go beyond the scope of just writing copy,
but actually driving new product ideas, new aspects of your business, new things you
want to do within it, or new ways you want to serve. So it is that powerful, I really hope
that it unlocks all those things for you. But what we're really getting at right now is just
unlocking the information and the insights and the understandings that are going to
help you be super steeped in the process and then even more so when you revisit them
later.
So the first section I want to look at is your relationship to your avatar. So simply why do
you do what you do? Here's the biggie. What about your market's struggles breaks your
heart? There was a time not too long ago when I was going through a period of
stagnation. I was speaking with one of my mentors at the time. His name is David
Wagner, he's just this awesome coach and yoga teacher and just does so many
awesome things, awesome work. He was coaching me and I told him, "I just don't really
have any intense desires or longings, you know? I'm feeling complacent, but I'm not
feeling like that's a problem. You know, like where else is there to go from here. I'm
cool, I'm fine." Then he instantly broke through that with one question. He just stopped
me, he said, "I hear you Ryan, but tell me, what breaks your heart more than anything?"
This just pulled me out from that mentation, this rationalization that I was okay. From
there, the bloodletting began. So you know, Hemingway once said, "Writing is easy, all
you have to do is puncture a vein and bleed onto the page." And a hundred years later
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 6 --

47 Golden Questions Walkthrough (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 2 of 6
what we're doing here is puncturing your heart and seeing what bleeds out onto the
pixels. And that is super powerful, I want you to stick with that. What about your market
struggles breaks your heart?
And what reality do you see for them, that they don't yet see for themselves? And what
pains you about their inability to see that potential? Another big one. What was your
biggest failure as a coach, educator, program creator? You know, how do you feel you
may have let your market down, and what did you learn from that experience? What
vows did you make from that moment? This is just extremely grounding to show that
you're not this perfect guru on a mountain, you know, but that you have your goals. You
have your goals in how you're able to serve. And when you can communicate that, why
you do what you do, how you failed before, you get them on your side to accomplish
what you're trying to achieve. So that kind of makes the buying decision less just about
them, and also it allows them to be empathetic and serve you through their purchase
decision, in a weird kind of cool way. So that's a big one.
What's the most gratifying or fulfilling experience you've had as a coach, educator, or
program creator? How did that experience exceed your expectations and maybe even
open up your eyes to a deeper impact that you didn't even know you were making? In
the thank you, welcome video after you enrolled, you saw the story of how a few years
ago someone once said, "Ryan, we're not saving lives." And that really, really pissed me
off, 'cause I take this seriously, and I knew there was a reason I take this seriously. It
wasn't until a few months ago when one of my clients sent me an email with the subject
line that said, "Your copy caused this." And it was just this amazing story of a woman
who was not going to be in the program, but then she joined based on one of the final
emails, and now she's achieving literally life-changing results and getting that
mentorship and guidance and education she needed. So that totally blew my
expectations of writing copy. So what is it about what you do, or what is that experience
that has exceeded your expectations? That could be a really big moment to connect
with your audience on.
And finally, like how deep in your commitment to serving this market? How have you re-
engineered your own life to create that impact? What have you sacrificed or given up in
the name of that mission? I write copy because I care about my ripple of impact more
than anything. There's only so much I could do under the name Ry Schwartz. But when I
lend these words, this copy, this marketing to all these amazing authors who are in a
place to do so much good in their own way, that is how my ripple of impact expands
beyond a reach that I can ever see. So yeah, talk about that in your marketing as well.
It's a connection point. And what have you learnt from your market? How have they
inspired you? Once again, this is very grounding and shows humility. And finally, the last
one for this section at least is the world will be a better place when ... And that really
kind of keys you in, once again it's another approach to why you do what you do.
Moving on, we have your avatar's perceived or real limitations. We're going to get into
this deeper later, so I'm just going kind of go quicker here, but what's the biggest excuse
your avatar makes for having not achieved a favorable outcome yet? How might that
excuse be justified? And maybe how can you even validate that excuse and say, "You're
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 6 --

47 Golden Questions Walkthrough (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 3 of 6
right. It wasn't your fault. This deck was stacked against you." And just really confirm
that. But the important thing is to follow that up with, what is about you or your
method that overcomes that limitation? So if you're going to validate it, that's powerful.
But you need to create this dynamic of, it's not your fault until it is. Because now you
can overcome that. How do your biggest competitors fall short in overcoming it? That's
another big one as far as positioning goes. What is a false belief they have about the
industry? What's a false belief that have about themselves and their abilities or
capacities to achieve it? What about the best way to solve the problem or achieve the
outcome? What false beliefs do they have around that?
Number eight is a really big one for me. I've used this for a lot of the big launches I've
done. But why might it be a good thing they waited until now? So especially if you're
teaching a topic that isn't particularly new, you're a little late to the game in teaching it.
You kind of want to show why it's a good thing they waited until now, too. You know,
you've waited a while to come out with your course, but why is it a good thing they've
waited until now to get in? I was helping Amy Porterfield sell her courses, Courses That
Convert course and her webinars, that Convert course. There were already tons of
courses on those topics, but we knew that our market out there wasn't getting those
results yet, or they haven't even attempted it. So we had to communicate why that
might have been a good thing. We tend to feel a lot of guilt around our procrastination,
because a lot of people procrasti-shame us. We procrasti-shame ourselves. So that's a
really amazing bonding experience when you can prove and show in a believable and
sincere way why it's a good thing they waited.
Then what are maybe the five biggest objections they might have around forging ahead
with your product? And for each, can you explore how you can create more comfort and
ease around it? Then here's a big one we're going to explore deeper. We already
touched upon it a bit in a previous exercise, but what are those sacred cows with sour
milk? So those commonly-held and widely-accepted beliefs and paradigms that don't
serve your prospects. So the ones they're most ready and willing to be released from.
Then we have what I call ethosgraphics. This goes beyond just demographics and
psychographics. This really goes down to what they believe, what they feel, how they
see themselves in this world. So what are tired of seeing? What makes them sick? So is
that marketing gurus with hyped-up promises and rented Lambos and all that scarcity
and urgency tactics, and 48 hours and 24 hours, and your life is going to be terrible if
you don't buy now. Like that's what makes our market really sick. You guys want to be
able to sell more, but you don't want to be able to just kind of lather on that [Foamo
00:11:15] that makes everyone feel queasy.
What is it about the way you run your business or program that will resonate with their
personal values? And one thing that is brilliant is what Ramit Sethi does, or at least he
used to do it, where he'd urge people not to incur credit card debt to enroll in his
programs. This really shows that preeminence that he's looking out for his audience,
that he doesn't want people to put themselves in vulnerable positions to work with him.
It sets that standard for his audience, but it also sets that standard for himself. And
people respect people who maintain these honorable values and qualities. So what is it
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 6 --

47 Golden Questions Walkthrough (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 4 of 6
about the way you run your business or program that will resonate with your audiences'
personal values?
How does your tribe inspire you to be better? Simple but powerful. Go there and
connect with them over those aspects of what you discover. What do you refuse to let
them be exposed to or endure anymore? You know I've said it before, but Foamo is
something you should be feeling when you feel their opportunity to escape exposure to
that thing is slipping. So what is that thing you refuse to let them expose to or endure
anymore? For me and in this course, I refuse to let skilled coaches and educators have
that funnel anxiety, or worse, start building funnels and product launches but never get
them off the ground because they realize there's this missing ingredient called email
copy. So that's what I refuse to let our audience and you guys be exposed to anymore.
But what is that in your business? In what other areas can you take a stand with them or
for them? Think of where competitors or common conventions have let them down and
left them feeling disillusioned. You want to break through that disillusionment and give
them hope again.
Moving on, these are some other powerful questions that have unlocked some pretty
cool things. I know that's a recurring theme here, but that's why they are here. That's
why there are 47 of them, because these are the 47 I go to. And maybe in a few months,
there will be 57. But for now, these are really the ones that I actually return to in every
launch.
Exclusive empowerment and self-forgiveness. What skills, characteristics, experiences,
circumstances, or points of pride exclusively empower your prospect to achieve the end
result? What missteps, regrets, or sunk costs might they need to forgive themselves for
before being willing to change? And this really plays into a lot of cognitive bias.
Sometimes making a decision to buy your program would mean admitting fault, at least
on a subtle, unconscious level that they messed up. And that could be painful. So you
want to actually facilitate that forgiveness, and there's a theme plate for that, market
search for meaning that you'll see a little latter if you have six-figure emails. How many
of those previous failures can be reframed as necessary steps or prerequisites for where
they are now? So that's where it comes full circle. You close that meaning loop. You
create meaning around those previous mess-ups or regrets, and you make it okay. You
make it part of that journey, part of that vindication.
Then we have what I call "neck brace" moments. These are where you get your prospect
nodding their head with excitement and just kind of saying, "Wow, someone finally gets
me." 'Cause it's just super gratifying and freeing to have our experience both verified
and validated to us. So these are the four questions that I go there. What can you
confirm to them about their present experience/struggle as it relates to the problem
your product solves? Just confirm their reality. Confirm what they're seeing. Can you
confirm to them why solving this really matters in their life? So if you can reflect back
their intention deeper than they're able to recognize it themselves, or deeper than
they're able to articulate it themselves, but they can still see that when it's reflected
back to them, that is a very powerful moment that builds trust and affinity.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 6 --

47 Golden Questions Walkthrough (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 5 of 6
What can you confirm to them about their skepticisms and reasons for being rightfully
skeptical? Once again, validation. And that's where the buyer's journey starts. It's in just
validating these pieces of their current context back to them. Where have they been
most let down or disillusioned? Because this is the thing that's going to give them the
most resistance around going on this journey. Like, "Oh no, not again, I've seen this
before." So how have they been most let down or disillusioned? And how can you really
cut through that?
Then we get into your product. This extends more into offer creation. If you've taken a
course on building your course or program, chances are you've seen some of these. But
in cast not, I do want to at least give you a chance to dive into them. So what is that
magic wand moment or outcome your product can deliver? Why is this important to
your prospect? Or why is it what they really need? And on an even deeper level, why
does that matter? And then on another even deeper level, why does that matter? And
I'm stumbling over my words because my mind is racing as I go down those levels, but I
want you to do the same. I want you to go down those rabbit holes, 'cause that's where
these discoveries are found.
What new opportunities will arise as a result? How do we frame this as a push goal that
opens them up to new favorable opportunities? Can we connect this outcome to other
important wants? For example, if you're teaching someone how to build an email list,
and that's your program, think of all the next outcomes they can now even pursue. They
can now create a product to sell to it, they can do affiliate promotions. They could ... it
just opens them up to all these possibilities that they've wanted to capitalize on in the
past but regretfully haven't been able to do so. So how can you frame the outcome of
your course as a push goal for other favorable opportunities?
What other intangible or unexpected problems does buying your product solve? One
thing I like to touch upon a lot is freeing them from the search. You know, how much
energy do we spend in comparing solutions and just being on the sidelines and watching
all these launches or opportunities go by, and that consumes a lot of energy. So freeing
someone from a search is a very powerful but intangible outcome. And there's
confidence to double down, you know, knowing that they're getting that return on
effort. This is so universal across everything we're selling. People aren't afraid of
spending time, they're afraid of wasting time. So giving someone a return on effort,
making them feel confident enough to double down on a solution is a very powerful,
unexpected outcome that a product can deliver.
Then we get into the four levels of benefits they'll experience using your product. I'll let
you go through these yourself, but essentially we could have the tangibles and we could
have the intangibles. And we have some better immediate and some better long term.
And just filling these all out will give you kind of a more holistic, broadened range of
what your product actually provides and more importantly, give you the chance to
reflect those back to your prospect.
Finally, this a very powerful, future-pacing exercise that I call the parallel
realities/divergent roads exercise, essentially. But in vivid detail, what does success in
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 6 --

47 Golden Questions Walkthrough (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 6 of 6
your program look like three days from now? So this is something they could still easily
visualize. They're probably wearing the same socks if they don't do laundry very often.
What does it look like in three weeks, three months, 12 months? So it gets harder and
harder, the future gets blurrier and blurrier, but if you can reflect that reality gradually
from three days, three weeks, then you earn that kind of believability and credibility
that you could know what it looks like it three months and 12 months, especially if
you've done your job and accurately reflected back the earlier ones.
Then in vivid detail to contrast that, what would their life look like three days from now
if they don't buy your product? Three weeks, three months, 12 months. Obviously we're
doing this with an agenda of proving to them beyond doubt that our product will offer
them the more favorable alternate reality or parallel reality. But you don't want to lose
credibility by exaggerating just how good, you know, be really honest and sincere of
what you think the reality will look like under both potential outcomes. So this is kind of
like that butterfly effect, you know, one with your product and one without it.
So with that said, we just did a full overview of the 47 questions. If you're doing it right
now, awesome, amazing. If not, you're going to certainly see them a little later. So at
least now, hopefully you've steeped in them a bit. You've gotten an idea of what we're
trying to do with all of them. Feel free to rewind back to this video in case you want to
just kind of give it a second look while you're doing it later.
So that's it for now. With that said, we are now ready to dive into module three.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
